Leading vacation rental marketplace: strong network effects strengthens economic moat. As at end-2022, ABNB had over 8mn global active listings and more than 5 mn hosts as at June 2024 which is telling of its strong brand position. The strong network effects are also evident in how prior guests remains the top source of hosts and guests also becomes hosts when they travel to other regions. We are of the view that ABNB’s position as a leading vacation rental marketplace will not be easily displaced given the strong network effects which brings together a pool of hosts and guests. Room night growth and OpEx discipline to drive EBITDA growth despite moderating ADRs. Consensus sees gross booking value increasing at 11%/12% in FY24/25, driven by 12%/12% growth in nights and experiences booked and moderating ADRs of -2%/-1% in FY24/FY25. We see downside risks to ADRs given changes in mix towards lower ADR regions, recently introduced pricing and discounting tools, as well as normalisation of record room rates. With its unique business model connecting hosts and guests, ABNB has an operationally lean model with low overheads. ABNB continues to stay well-disciplined with costs, as seen by a 5% decline in headcount despite a 75% growth in revenue (2022 compared to 2019). Accordingly, EBITDA should come in 12%/15% higher in FY24/FY25, implying operating EBITDA margin of 36%/37% for FY24/FY25. Further moderations in 3Q24 expected on signs of tapering demand in the US. We see room for downward revision in consensus forecasts (USD3.84bn) to be in line with 3Q24 revenue guidance of USD 3.67-3.73bn (+8-10% y/y). The group expects “sequential moderation” in growth of nights and experiences in 3Q24 as the booking window shortens and travel demand in the US slows. We believe that a contraction in the booking lead time is indicative of consumers’ lower propensity to spend on travel given the economic uncertainty. Despite expecting a modest y/y increase in ADRs in 3Q24, management is guiding for flattish adjusted EBITDA on a y/y basis in 3Q24 on higher marketing investment to support new growth markets (vs consensus growth of +9%). Overall, we believe that the risks are skewed towards the downside given slowing domestic travel in North America which accounts for the vast majority of nights booked within the region. Upgrade to BUY with unchanged TP USD165. We roll over our TP to 19x CY26 EV/EBITDA, which is close to -0.5SD of the historical mean. Despite tougher y/y comparisons expected in 2H25 due to the strong second half of last year, we believe the new verticals in services and experiences could become meaningful long-term contributors. Early adoption metrics for services such as high guest satisfaction, strong host interest, and seamless app integration indicate a credible path to scale and support long-term monetization potential. Consistent execution in expansion markets also confirms product market fit and brand success in new regions, which could help diversify revenue concentration risk outside North America.

Risks: Macroeconomic slowdown. Recession and macro uncertainty may impact discretionary spending on travel and could negatively impact travel service providers through fewer bookings and rates. Intensifying competition. ABNB competes with both online and traditional travel service providers such as Booking Holdings and Expedia. Players such as Google are also expanding into the travel booking space by offering services such as Google Hotel Ads, Google Flights, and Google Travel, adding competitive pressure to the industry.

gt_prediction_text:
Despite recent challenges, a formidable presence in its core markets underscores enduring dominance and strategic resilience. Boeing holds a dominant position in the commercial aerospace sector, operating as part of a duopoly with Airbus. Together, they produce the majority of the world’s narrowbody and widebody aircraft, a market characterised by demand consistently outpacing supply. Despite recent production challenges, airlines are compelled to remain with Boeing, as Airbus’s production slots are booked into the next decade. In the highly regulated defence sector, Boeing is one of the top five prime contractors for the US and allied nations. With its extensive product range and deep expertise in driving innovation, Boeing is poised to capitalise on secular growth opportunities across both sectors. Earnings and cash flow inflection from 2HFY25, as production stabilises and deliveries accelerate. Boeing remains on track for recovery in FY25, with 737 production set to exceed 38 per month later this year, reaching 42 by year-end, while 787 output rises to 7 per month. The Spirit AeroSystems acquisition, tighter supply chain oversight, production streamling and process improvement should enhance execution, while inventory liquidation and the wind- down of shadow factories will support cash flow. Although 4QFY24 highlighted ongoing operational challenges, we believe increasing deliveries and a more stable production cadence will drive a turnaround in earnings and cash flow in 2H25, setting the stage for stronger financial performance. Improving free cash flow trajectory should drive a re-rating, despite near-term credit concerns. Boeing’s USD3.5bn cash burn in 4QFY24 reflects working capital pressures and production inefficiencies, but free cash flow should turn positive in 2H25, as deliveries ramp up and supply chain bottlenecks ease. While the USD10bn free cash flow goal will likely be pushed to FY27, we expect progressive deleveraging from FY25 onwards, easing credit overhang concerns. Execution risks remain, particularly in defence contract overruns and regulatory approvals for the 737 MAX, but with demand fundamentals intact and improving operational stability, we see meaningful upside as Boeing regains investor confidence through sustained cash flow recovery. We rate Boeing a BUY with a TP of USD260.0. Our TP is based on 25x FY27 EPS (discounted back), which is around 2SD above its 5-year pre-crisis average, and at a moderate premium to the sector average of 20-22x. We justify this premium based on Boeing's recovery from a low base, coupled with its strong positioning for sustained growth into the next decade, contingent on successful execution.

Risks: Ongoing 737 MAX production issues risk delivery delays and cash flow slippage. Tariff-related cost increases could erode margins if mitigation proves insufficient. Analyst Jason Sum | groupresearch@dbs.com

Transiting to be the world’s smallest bottler through franchise model. Coca-Cola operates a unique franchise distribution network, investing in international bottling partners through its Bottling Investment Group and later divesting its equity stakes once they become successful. It has over 950 bottling plants across 200 bottling partners worldwide. Post-divestment, these partners retain exclusive rights to bottle Coca-Cola products, paying a franchise fee and remaining contractually obligated to purchase concentrates from the company. This model ensures high-margin revenue while monetising bottling stakes. Maintained guidance for organic revenue growth of 5% to 6% and raised USD EPS growth from 2% to 3% to 3% for FY25. Management projects 5%–6% organic revenue growth and 2%–3% EPS growth for FY25. Growth will be price/mix-driven (3%–5%), while volume growth is expected at 1%–2%, aligning with consensus estimates. Price/mix impact is expected to moderate, given easing inflation in hyperinflationary markets and Fairlife’s slowing growth. Management is optimistic about volume, aiming for the upper end of guidance (2%). As of 1H25, the entire 5% organic growth was driven by price/mix with concentrate volume flat y/y while unit case volume was up 1% y/y. With strong profit growth in 1H25, the company plans to reinvest part of the gains into marketing initiatives. With broadly supportive underlying trends and stepped-up investment in 2H, management remains confident in accelerating volume growth in 2H25. Well-defined strategies in place to navigate headwinds and support continued growth. Coca- Cola has clear strategies to mitigate challenges and sustain growth. For instance, it used generative AI to create its 4Q24 Christmas ad, reducing costs and turnaround time. Regarding the 25% aluminium tariff, management expects a manageable cost impact through packaging adjustments (shifting towards PET over aluminium) and hedging strategies. On the balance sheet, net debt stands at 1.8x EBITDA, rising to 2.0x EBITDA post-Fairlife contingent consideration payment but remaining at the lower end of its 2x–2.5x target range. Share buybacks have been slow, with USD4.8bn in repurchase headroom, partly to support the Fairlife consideration payout and potentially prepare for an adverse IRS tax appeal outcome, which could take years to resolve. Lift TP to USD83 (prev: USD77). We applied a higher valuation of 28x, +3S.D. of its 5-year average forward PE, on its FY25F earnings. We believe the higher valuation premium is justified given its positioning as one of the few rare blue chip consumer staple names relatively insulated from current trade tensions with its highly localised production and well-executed all-weather strategy.

Risks: Key risk includes uncertainty surrounding the IRS tax dispute appeal, but a resolution is likely years away.

Leader in low-cost portfolio. ConocoPhillips (COP) continues to focus on generating low cost-of-supply portfolio by transitioning its well drilling, casing, and completion design. It has also undertaken property management to increase efficiency, as well as acquired businesses like Concho Resources and Shell’s Permian assets over the years to establish a leading position in the prolific Permian basin. With record production of 1.99mmboepd achieved in 2024, COP has guided for around 18–20% production growth in 2025 to 2.34– 2.38mmboepd, driven by organic growth as well as the completion of landmark acquisition of Marathon Oil in late 2024. Production guidance has been maintained at the end of 1H25, despite the planned USD1.3bn sale of Anadarko Basin assets, closing early 4Q25. Growing its core oil and LNG portfolios. 2025 capex budget has been moderated to USD12.3-12.6bn from c.USD12.9bn but this is still higher than 2024 levels of USD11.5bn, driven by continued growth in Alaska & Canada (USD2.0bn) and on track to delivering low single-digit organic production growth. Total company and Lower 48 production in 3Q25 reached 2,399mboepd (2Q25: 2,391mboepd) and 1,528mboepd (2Q25: 1,508mboepd) respectively, which reflects the Marathon Oil integration. The LNG strategy continues to scale, adding 5 MTPA of offtake for a ~10 MTPA portfolio, with North Field East, North Field South and PALNG on track for first LNG in 2026, while LNG project capital has been cut to USD3.4bn after a USD0.6bn credit against Port Arthur spend. Meanwhile, Willow (Alaska) capex estimates was raised by USD1.5bn to USD8.5–9.0bn range due to inflation, localised North Slope and marine cost escalation, which could be concerning to shareholders, given the project is already pricey. Though first oil from Willow was narrowed to early 2029, keeping the ~USD4.0bn FCF inflection intact, progress will be keenly watched here. Enhanced shareholder return guidance. COP has a strong balance sheet position and has committed to return at least 30% of its cash from operations to shareholders, with more usual 40-45% actual payout range. For 9M25, distributions have totalled USD6.9bn, on track to beat 2024 level of USD9.1bn. Distributions will roughly be split 40:60 between dividend and share buybacks. This implies close to 9.5% total distribution yield at current prices. Given the macro uncertainties, commitment to this level of distributions for the year will be a strong support for the share price. Preliminary 2026 guidance points to 0–2% underlying production growth, with capex at USD12bn (USD0.5bn below 2025 midpoint) and adjusted operating costs easing further (USD0.4bn lower y/y), supported by lower major project capital, Marathon Oil synergies, and cost reduction programme. We have a BUY call on COP with a TP of USD118. COP’s share price performance has been affected by recent volatility in oil prices. However, we reckon oil prices will hold up in 2H25, and given the strong earnings delivery and shareholder returns commitment, we reiterate our BUY rating on COP, with an unchanged TP of USD118. Our TP is based on a forward P/BV multiple of 2.3x, which is at +0.5 S.D. levels historically.

Risks: COP is exposed to significant macroeconomic risks as earnings depend on the price volatility of crude oil, natural gas and other refinery products. Further, COP is exposed to significant legal and regulatory risk as energy industry is highly regulated in different jurisdictions across the world. The Willow project in Alaska has been mired in controversy owing to environmental concerns and it remains to be seen whether the development can progress without delays in future.
